coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical immunosensing by carbon ink/carbon dot/ZnO-labeled-Ag@polypyrrole composite biomarker for CA-125 ovarian cancer detection
coredata.dc:creator: Krathumkhet, Nattinee
Imae, Toyoko
Wang, Fu-ming
Yuan, Chiou-Chung
Manidae Lumban Gaol, Jusni
Paradee, Nophawan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we demonstrated a novel cancer antigen 125 (CA125) biomarker detection based on electrochemical immunosensor. The biomarker on conductive composite materials of carbon ink/carbon dot/zine oxide (C-ink/CD/ZnO) was employed as an electrode platform by using ITO substrate to enhance the interaction of antibodies (Ab) with supporting catalytic performance of ZnO as a labeling signal molecule. They were a scientist attention for biosensor with chemical stability, strong biocompatibility, high conductive signal, and accuracy. Moreover, the nanocomposite of silver@polypyrrole (Ag@PPy) was used as a potential redox mediator. The labeled construction with Ag@PPy was more accuracy than that of a free-labeled. The created immunosensor was a wide linear range as 1 ag·mL−1 − 100 ng·mL−1 and a low limitation of detection as 0.1 fg·mL−1 under the optimal condition. This suggested that the immunosensor is considered to be an accurate and efficient diagnostic tool for CA125 and other biomarkers detection in actual sample analysis for clinic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cancer antigen 125
Electrochemical immunosensor
Ovarian cancer
Silver@polypyrrle nanoparticles
Zinc oxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon ink (C-ink), carbon dots (CD) and zinc oxide (ZnO) materials
Analytical performance of the electrochemical sensor
Real test analyst in human serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research project was supported by King Mongkut's University of Technology Thonburi under KMUTT Research Center of Excellence Project and Postdoctoral Fellowship, and Nanoarchitecture and Nanotechnology group (NTUST), for a Visiting scholarship. The authors would like to acknowledge Fitriani Jati Rahmania for XRD data analyses and Chen Wei-ling for preparation of human serum.

This research project was supported by King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi under KMUTT Research Center of Excellence Project and Postdoctoral Fellowship, and Nanoarchitecture and Nanotechnology group (NTUST), for a Visiting scholarship. The authors would like to acknowledge Fitriani Jati Rahmania for XRD data analyses and Chen Wei-ling for preparation of human serum.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra, (B) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, (C) XPS, (D) Contact angle, SEM and EDS of C-ink, C-ink/CD, and C-ink/CD/ZnO and (E) TEM of ZnO.
(A) IR spectra and (B) XRD pattern of Ag@PPy, (C) Zeta potential and (D) TEM, SEM and EDS of Ag@PPy and Ag@PPy/PEG.
DPV detection of (A) free-labeled (Ab-Ag) with R2 = 0.77 and (B) labeled (Ab-Ag-Ab) types with R2 = 0.92.
(A) Calibration plot concentration of CA125 vs peak current (mA) and DPV response of electrochemical immunoassay for CA125 in human serum.

Preparation steps of materials for CA-125 immunosensor. (A) Synthesis of C-ink and (B) CD. (C) Combined ofAg@PPy/PEG and Ab2 with covalence bond. (D) Electrode of ITO/C-ink/CD/ZnO/Ab1 as free-labeled. (E) Construction ofITO/C-ink/CD/ZnO/CA-125 labeled with Ag@PPy/PEG/Ab2.
Comparison table for different types of immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical immunosensing by carbon ink/carbon dot/ZnO-labeled-Ag@polypyrrole composite biomarker for CA-125 ovarian cancer detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we demonstrated a novel cancer antigen 125 (CA125) biomarker detection based on electrochemical immunosensor. The biomarker on conductive composite materials of carbon ink/carbon dot/zine oxide (C-ink/CD/ZnO) was employed as an electrode platform by using ITO substrate to enhance the interaction of antibodies (Ab) with supporting catalytic performance of ZnO as a labeling signal molecule. They were a scientist attention for biosensor with chemical stability, strong biocompatibility, high conductive signal, and accuracy. Moreover, the nanocomposite of silver@polypyrrole (Ag@PPy) was used as a potential redox mediator. The labeled construction with Ag@PPy was more accuracy than that of a free-labeled. The created immunosensor was a wide linear range as 1 ag·mL−1 − 100 ng·mL−1 and a low limitation of detection as 0.1 fg·mL−1 under the optimal condition. This suggested that the immunosensor is considered to be an accurate and efficient diagnostic tool for CA125 and other biomarkers detection in actual sample analysis for clinic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a disease caused when the normal cells transform into the tumor cells in a multi-stage phase and spread into surrounding organs [1,2]. Ovarian cancer (OC) is a type of cancer which is the fifth biggest cause of morbidity and mortality from cancer. Highest risk could be more potentially occurred for women in aged more than fifty years [3,4]. In general, the diagnosed case of OC can be separated into four-stage within a survival rate of around five-year. Early-stage (I, II), the OC patients have a 90 % survival rate, while the late-stage (III, IV) OC patients have only a 20–40 % survival rate. The OC patients have a five-year survival rate of 45 %, given progress in therapy [5]. Therefore, the enhancing patient recovery or improving patient survival is early detection and timely treatment of initial diagnosis [6]. Recently, several researchers and manufacture have attracted a great interest in more effective therapy in order to reduce the possibility of normal cells being damaged or killed [7].

A biosensor is a device involving in a diagnostic system that is converted to electrical signal from a chemical reaction or biological response for using in biomarkers measured in all organ and tissue cells. It is a modern clinical device which is a potential to detect active cancers and efficient treatment at an early stage [8]. The approach used to identify the presence of the tumor marker cancer antigen 125 (CA125) in the blood is typically diagnosed ovarian cancer [9]. The CA125 has been defined as an effective biomarker for OC. It is also calledas mucin 16 (MUC16) and it is found in human serum more than 35 U mL−1 by the MUC16 gene [10]. For the detection of CA 125, the variety of analytical methods have been investigated including electrochemical immunoassay,enzyme-linked immunoassay, solid-phase sandwich enzyme immunoassay, radioimmunoassay, electrochemiluminescence immunosensors, fluorescence immunosensors,and piezoelectric immunosensors [11]. These approaches require particularly involved assay protocols such as an antibody (Ab) or antigen (Ag) labeling.

Electrochemical is one of the most promising analysis for biomarker detection because it has been attracting features such as an easy procedures, high sensitivity, fast detection and the ability to be miniaturized [12]. Charge transfer resistance and capacitance properties are working to enhance the accuracy and sensitivity of electrochemical technique to improve the bioreceptor transducer [13]. The improvement of immunosensor performance, the nanoparticles and nanomaterials have been studied and gained significant attention and remarkable diagnostic for cancer in recent years [14].

Nanoscale with advancement feature can enhance the unique physicochemical properties for electrical properties, unique surface area, particle size shape, dispersity, and surface change as well as improvement of interaction between electrodes and enzyme. They are commonly used in biosensor including metal nanostructures, carbon-based nanomaterials, and polymer hybrid nanometerials [15]. Metal nanostructures, gold (Au) [16], platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd) [17], zinc oxide (ZnO), and silver (Ag) have been widely used in analytical and biological applications because of their accuracy and convenience of production, high conductivity, and ability to improve the quantity of labeling enzyme sensitivity and enhance analytical signal detection in electrochemical biosensors [18,19].

Additionally, carbon nanomaterials have been interesting possibilities for enhancing sensor performance. They are increasingly being used in biosensor platforms in a great potential area of biosensor research. Carbon nanomaterials namely single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT), multiple-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), carbon nanohorn (CNH), graphite (GR), graphene oxide (GO), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) are commonly employed, while carbon dots (CD) are growing as novel nanomaterials for sensing fabrication and unique particle sizes around 5 nm with useful for biological cell immobilization [20].

At this present work, we fabricated an electrochemical immunosensor OC detection based on sandwich structure ITO/C-ink/CD/ZnO electrodes as immunosensor for early cancer diagnosis. In relation to improve electron transfer of carbon ink (C-ink)/CD composite electrodes, ZnO was offered to support the electrochemical performance. Next, to produce a sandwich immunosensor, Ag nanoparticles modified through polypyrrole and PEG (Ag@PPy/PEG) conjugated with target antibody were applied to promote the antibody deposition and antigen detection sensitivity.

Nafion (perfluorinated resin) solution (5 wt% in lower aliphatic alcohols and 15–20 % water) and hexadecytrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Graphite flake (4 μm in size) was purchased from ITO Company, Japan. Silver nitrate was supplied by Alfa Aesar, USA. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 99 %), citric acid, ethylenediamine (99 %), sulfuric acid (98 %), pyrrole (99 %), l-cystine, sodium metasilicate (SMS) and ammonium persulfate (APS, 98 %) were received from Acros Organics, UK. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.4), and serum CA125 antigen, X75 and X306 antibodies were purchased from UNI-ONWARD (New Taipei City, Taiwan). The clonality of X75 (M 11-like) and X306 (OC 125-like) anti CA125 antibodies was monoclonal and hosted by a mouse. AimCore Technology supplied the indium tin oxide (ITO) glass. In all syntheses, deionized water purified by a Millipore purification system (Yamato Millipore WT100) with a resistivity of 18.2 M.cm was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon ink (C-ink), carbon dots (CD) and zinc oxide (ZnO) materials
ce:section.ce:para: C-ink was prepared through a reduction method. Sodium metasilicate (500 mg) was dispersed in water (50 ml). Then gradually added graphite (7 g) was homogeneously mixed together under vigorously stirring. The obtained mixture solution was become homogeneous solutions.

CD material was synthesized through a condensation and carbonization with hydrothermal process. Citric acid (1 g) was dissolved in water (50 ml) while stirring continuously, and ethylenediamine (300 L) was added to the solution while stirring until it formed a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the precursor solution was moved into an autoclave, slowly. Finally, the solution was heated by autoclave with maintained temperature at 230 ˚C for 5 h. The solution was obtained with brown and transparent.

ZnO was prepared by the same as studied with polyol method [21]. The zinc nitrate hexahydrate (15.90 g) and NaOH (2.00 g) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (50 ml) under continuous stirring, separately. After 30 min, NaOH solution was poured into zinc nitrate hexahydrate solution and stirred at 80 °C for 1 h.

The sandwich layers were prepared by cleaned working electrode of ITO glass (1 × 2 cm2/piece) sonicated in acetone and ethanol with 1:1 v/v for 30 min and washed with water and then treated by ultraviolet light (172 nm) under vacuum. The mixture of C-ink/CD/ZnO at concentration of 1:0.75:0.25 v/v with 300 μl were mixed a nafion/EtOH (1:1 v/v, 100 μl). The mixture solution (60 μl) was coated on ITO under active area of 1 × 1 cm2. Then, EDC:NHS (1:1 M ratio) was prepared by added EDC (60 μl) and following added NHS (60 μl) under continuously stirring for 6 h at room temperature. After that, l-cystine in 0.1 M HCl (10 mM, 60 μl) was functionalized on electrode and allowed to react for 12 h.

The activation stock solution for the –NH2 group in anti-CA125. An amide bond was formed when the activated –NH2 group linked with the carboxyl group of l-cysteine. To detach unbound antibodies, the antibody-covered electrode was washed in PBS. Following that, the free binding sites were inhibited for 30 min using 1 % BSA. The electrode was washed several times with PBS before being kept at 4 °C for use in the experiment [22].

Synthesis of Ag@ PPy was made through in-situ polymerization. Typically, solution of silver nitrate (120 mg) and pyrrole monomer (30 μl) were added in water (20 ml). Then CTAB (124 mg) and oxidizing agent namely APS (204 mg) was slowly added into an aqueous 0.1 M HCl (10 ml) solution and stirred overnight at 4 °C. The precipitate collected was washed and filtered three times with ethanol and water, and then dried at 40 °C for 24 h to obtain a powder as a core–shell structure [23]. The Ag@PPy/PEG was provided by absorption method of Ag@PPy (100 mg) mixed PEG 400 (500 µl) under sonicated for 2 h. Next, the conjugated with covalent bond of Ag@PPy/PEG (10 mg) bonded with antibody-CA125 (20 mg/ml, 1 ml) solutions was combined with shaker for 1 h.

The ITO/C-ink/CD/ZnO/Ab1/CA125-modified the electrode was incubated with (10-7 − 10-18 g/ml) of CA125 for 60 min at 30 °C for antibody–antigen reaction. To eliminate the linked antigens, the electrode was cleaned. Then, ITO/C-ink/CD/ZnO/Ab1/CA125 electrode was incubated in Ag@PPy/PEG/Ab2 at room temperature for 1 h to form sandwich structure as shown in Scheme 1 .

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) investigations were performed to determine the material's sensitivity and accuracy by (Zennium - ZAHNER®, Germany) electrode materials used in the three-electrode system, such as the working electrode, a Pt wire as a counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. A FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, Nexus 6700, Japan) was used to analyze the infrared absorption spectra for KBr pellets over the course of 500 scans. A UV–visible spectrophotometer (V-670, Jasco, Japan) with a 1 cm-path cell was used to measure the ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectra. The zeta potential was investigated on a HORIBA Scientific and Photal Instrument (SZ-100, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Rigaku D/Max-kA diffractometer with CuKα radiation at 40 kV and 30 mA was mesured at 10-80° and a step of 4 min−1. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-6390, Japan) was operated by 120 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VGScientific, ESCALAB 250, England) was conducted. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM and EDS, JEOL JSM-6500F, Japan) was perform by 15 kV. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method was investigated after activation of materials by N2 adsorption − desorption isotherms on a BELSORPmax (Bel, Japan). All samples were pre-heating at 300 °C for 3 h before the measurement.

Fig. 1 (A) shows IR absorption spectra of the C-ink/CD/ZnO composite materials. The broad band of ZnO at 3435 cm−1 and bands at 1560, 1500, 1381, and 465 cm−1 were corresponded to the OH, CH, CH, and Zn − O vibrations, respectively. The bands of CD at 3435, 2945, 1650, 1553, and 1389 cm−1 were indicated to O H, C H, CO, N H, and CC vibration band which were originated from the carboxylic acid, and amine groups of graphitic structure, respectively [24]. The bands of C-ink/CD/ZnO at 3435, 2940, 1650, 1553, and 1381 cm−1 were corresponded to O H, C H, CO, N H/CC, and Zn − O stretching, respectively.

The isotherms of C-ink and C-ink/CD/ZnO are plotted in Fig. 1 . (B). The results showed a nonporous structure of type II curve, and their surface areas were 7.4 and 1.9 m2/g for C-ink and C-ink/CD/ZnO, respectively. The successful fabrication of the nanocomposite between CD and ZnO on C-ink was proven due to decreasing surface area of C-ink/CD/ZnO composite.

The elemental compositions of C-ink, C-ink/CD and C-ink/CD/ZnO were analyzed from XPS analysis. The XPS result was revealed the existence of Zn2p (1021.45 eV) peak besides N1s (401.32 eV), O1s (531.84 eV) and C1s (284.89 eV) peaks in material composites are presented Fig. 1 . (C).

The presence of CD and ZnO enhanced the water absorption ability was a significant influence on the electrode surface. It was ahydrophilic characteristic because of the contenting substituent of OH functional groupfrom CD and ZnO, as demonstrated in EDS observations. When compared CD and ZnO, it could be seen ZnO more absorbed water than that of CD that mean a successful fabrication of material composites. The structure and morphology of C-ink, C-ink/CD and C-ink/CD/ZnO were measured by SEM as shown in Fig. 1 . (D). The SEM images showed the wrinkled paper-like and lamellar structure of C-ink. The C-ink/CD/ZnO showed the homogeneous material composites which revealed that the graphite sheets of C-ink were covered and contributed by CD and ZnO particles. The ZnO particles on C-ink showed the particles size around 20 nm that were confirmed via TEM image as shown in Fig. 1 . (E).

The effect of PPy concentration on Ag was evaluated with IR spectra. The addition of silver nitrate to the process, the significant nitrate doping vibrations were seen at a wavenumber around 1385 cm−1. The composite of Ag@PPy bands at 3450 and 1545 cm−1 were corresponded to NH starching and CC starching of aromatic ring of polypyrrole. The peak at 1385 cm−1 was indicated to CN starching of Ag of Ag@PPy composites as presented in Fig. 2
(A) [25].

XRD pattern of Ag@PPy was shown a broad peak around 2θ value of 20° which was the characteristic diffraction of amorphous polypyrrole, besides the unique diffraction peaks for silver at 2θ values 38.2°, 44.3°, 64.5°and 77.4°. These peaks were indexed to the diffraction planes as (210), (111), (200), (220), (311) of pure face centered cubic silver as shown in Fig. 2 . (B) [26].

The Ag@PPy was showed zeta potential of positive charge at 24.00 ± 1.36 mV. After the addition of PEG showed a negative charge at –23.86 ± 2.42 mV that was confirmed interaction of Ag@PPy/PEG as shown in Fig. 2 . (C).

In TEM images, the size distributions were established by sample particles. The average particle sizes was 100 ± 5.0 nm for Ag@PPy. Also, TEM could be seen the morphologies of Ag@PPy/PEG core–shell structure after combined with PEG and represented characteristic round sharp as shown in SEM images. It found that the characteristic of Ag@PPy showed black particles, however, the addition of PEG showed the characteristic round sharp of clear appearance outside Ag@PPy particle. The combination of PEG with Ag@PPy was achieved for synthesis with increasing oxygen content as shown in EDS in Fig. 2 . (D).

The obtained electrodes performance in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3 including 0.1 M KCl was investigated in this experiment to assess the electrochemical characteristics of the electrode. The results showed that the addition of CD showed the significant increase of current up to C-ink:CD 1:0.75 v/v as shown in Fig. S1
(A). The C-ink/CD/ZnO at 1:0.75:0.25 v/v modified electrode was exhibited an excellent electrochemical property with ZnO Fig. S1
(B). Therefore, the C-ink/CD/ZnO at 1:0.75:0.25 v/v was selected for the modification of the electrode. The CV was used to evaluate the stepwise creation of the immunosensor and to consider the interface characteristics of the electrode material Fig. S1
(C). The anti-CA125 Ab was attached to the surface of the modified electrode, which resulted in a considerable reduction in redox current. Ab was immobilized by lowering the peak current which reduced the electron movement between the electrode surface and the redox probe. After that, BSA was added as a blocker to avoid nonspecific binding, and CA125 Ag was lowered at each phase. Overall, these present may indicate that the immunosensor had been formed.

To maximize experimental results under free- labeled settings CA125 detection, the DPV tests were performed through the electrochemical detection of CA125 antigen by using [Fe (CN)6] 3 redox probe as the signal. The pH of PBS electrolytes affected the electrode and the biological activity and the immunosensor respond signal are depicted in Fig. S2
(A) and (B). The currents experiment of electrochemical in PBS at various pH values (5.0–8.0) showed that the increasing pH values up to 6.0 substantially increased the current signal in both of electrode and the biological activity. The current signal was reduced when the pH was raised from 6.0 to 8.0. Thus, the pH 6.0 was maximum value and was selected for using in whole experiment. The optimum incubation temperature for response to antibody and antigen was tested in various temperatures (4, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C). The results showed that at 30 °C was the best condition for promoting antibody and antigen interaction availability as shown in Fig. S2
(D). The incubation times were also optimized. The results demonstrated that 60 min was the appropriate incubation time as seen in Fig. S2
(C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the electrochemical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 . (A) and (B) display DPV detection of free-labeled (Ab-Ag) and labeled (Ab-Ag-Ab) of CA125 detection respectively under optimized conditions. The results can be seen that linear regression of labeled was better than free-labeled method. The labeled type may help provide strong interaction of Ab-Ag linkage. Current on DPV curves was discovered to have a linear relationship with CA concentration 125 within the range of 1 ag•mL−1 – 100 ng•mL−1 with the linear regression. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula LOD = 3 × S.D./sensitivity, where S.D is the standard deviation of the blank and sensitivity is the slope of the regression equation [22]. The LOD was showed at 0.1 fg·mL−1 (S/N = 3). These findings showed that the constructed biosensor was outstanding analytical performance. The acquired analytical performance was also compared to that of a previously published immunosensor, as shown in Table 1 . When compared to other immunosensors, the analytical results were preferred. The detection limit, in particular, was improved with wide linear ranges and low LOD.

MOF: metal–organic frame work, CNT: carbon nanotube, Tyr: tyrosinase, ZnO: zinc oxide, rGO: reduced graphene oxide, AuNPs: gold nanoparticles, GO: Graphene oxide, MPO: Myeloperoxidase, 3DrGO: three-dimensional reduced graphene oxide, MWCNT: multiwall carbon nanotube, PAMAM: polyamidoamine, Suc-CS: succinyl-chitosan, MNP: magnetic nanoparticles, TB: toluidine blue, PSA: prostate specific antigen, Pd NPs: palladium nanoparticles, C-ink: carbon ink, CD: carbon dot, Ag: silver, PPy: polypyrrole, DPV: differential pulse voltammetry, SWV: square wave voltammetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real test analyst in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition determined approach was used to assess the usability of the presented immunosensor in clinical conditions within the human blood serum samples collected from five patients (two samples with OC were tested from Taipei Medical University Shuang Ho Hospital). The CA125 was added to the diluted serums in the various concentrations. The construction of biomarker, as illustrated in Fig. 4 , had a lot of potential for determining CA125 in realistic human serum samples. In Fig. 4 . (A) and (B) were corresponded to CA125 diagnosis that showed linear relationship while Fig. 4 . (C), (D) and (E) were not related with CA125 which presented non-linear relationship. The evidence of the standard addition indicated that the constructed immunosensor was a promising technique used to detect CA125 in human serum and detecting it early staged of the disease.

This work successfully fabricated material composites of electrode and their ability to measure CA125 were reported. The characterization of labeled electrochemical immunosensor method was more accurate than free-labeled method detection of CA125 antigen. The preparation of label of electrode including ZnO/CD functionalized C-ink composite and constructed with Ag@PPy as sensor platform with enhancing the excellent chemical stability and good linear correlation 1 ag·mL−1 − 100 ng·mL−1. The low detection limit of 0.1 fg·mL−1 was achieved for detecting CA125 with reliability (S/N = 3). Overall, the developed immune sensor appears to be a promising breakthrough for OC biomarker in the clinic based on its excellent CA125 detection performance in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, selectivity, analysis, and low cost.

Nattinee Krathumkhet: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Toyoko Imae: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Fu-ming Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Chiou-Chung Yuan: Methodology, Investigation. Jusni Manidae Lumban Gaol: Methodology, Investigation. Nophawan Paradee: Supervision, Conceptualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research project was supported by King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi under KMUTT Research Center of Excellence Project and Postdoctoral Fellowship, and Nanoarchitecture and Nanotechnology group (NTUST), for a Visiting scholarship. The authors would like to acknowledge Fitriani Jati Rahmania for XRD data analyses and Chen Wei-ling for preparation of human serum.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioelechem.2023.108430.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural killer cells in cancer biology and therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Wu, T. Fu, Y.Z. Jiang, Z.M. Shao, Natural killer cells in cancer biology and therapy, Mol Cancer.7 (2020) 2-26. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12943-020-01238-x

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The beneficial effects of metformin on cancer prevention and therapy: a comprehensive review of recent advances
